rule: For the purposes of these rules, "health care professional" includes any licensed or unlicensed individual, student, volunteer, or non-clinical employee performing tasks in support of patient care at a health care facility.
"Health care facility" includes any hospital, nursing home, pharmacy, blood bank, or temporary facility authorized to provide health care services.

"Health care services in support of the State's COVID-19 response" includes clinical, administrative, and logistical acts or omissions, including the management of non-COVID patients, that are influenced by pandemic-related resource allocation, facility policies, or staffing shortages.

Notwithstanding any provision of the Connecticut General Statutes or any other state law, including the common law, or any associated regulations, rules, policies, or procedures, any health care professional or health care facility shall be immune from suit for civil liability for any injury or death alleged to have been sustained because of the individual's or health care facility's acts or omissions undertaken in good faith while providing health care services in support of the State's COVID-19 response.

Such immunity includes but is not limited to acts or omissions undertaken because of a lack of resources, including staffing, equipment, or PPE, attributable to the COVID-19 pandemic, that renders the health care professional or health care facility unable to provide the level or manner of care that otherwise would have been required in the absence of the COVID-19 pandemic and which resulted in the damages at issue. This includes errors resulting from fatigue or the use of experimental or expired materials when necessitated by resource scarcity.

Such immunity shall not extend to acts or omissions that constitute a crime, fraud, malice, gross negligence, willful misconduct, or would otherwise constitute a false claim or prohibited act pursuant to Section 4-275 et seq. of the Connecticut General Statutes or 31 U.S.C. §§3729 et seq. Decisions regarding the allocation of scarce resources, such as triage protocols or redirecting staff, shall not, by themselves, constitute malice, gross negligence, or willful misconduct.

Nothing in this order shall remove or limit any immunity conferred by any provision of the Connecticut General Statutes or other law. In the event of a conflict between the immunity provided by this order and any other law, the provision providing the broader immunity shall control.
